blocks.text: Chapter 5.
Updating: Many Small Steps, Few Big Leaps
The ideas in 30 seconds

Superforecasters revise their forecasts far more often than average forecasters, and updating frequency is one of the strongest statistical predictors of accuracy — but the typical revision is small, often just a few percentage points.

Skilled updating requires beating two opposite failure modes: underreaction, where commitment and identity make people defend stale beliefs, and overreaction, where irrelevant or noisy news yanks estimates around.

The engine of good updating is Bayesian in spirit: your new belief should equal your prior belief adjusted by how diagnostic the new evidence is — how much more likely it would be if one hypothesis were true rather than another.

Superforecasters rarely compute Bayes' theorem explicitly; they internalize its logic, as Bill Flack did when he discounted a headline-grabbing Russian announcement about Arafat's remains because it barely bore on the actual question.
Small steps are the default because they average out errors, but dogmatic gradualism is its own trap: when evidence is highly diagnostic, the right move is a large, decisive jump.

Updating cannot rescue a bad starting point — superforecasters' initial estimates were already roughly 50% more accurate than regular forecasters', so both the first judgment and every revision matter.
Frequent, Tiny Revisions Are a Signature of Accuracy

A natural suspicion is that superforecasters win simply because they are news junkies with more time to read. The Good Judgment Project data tell a subtler story. Superforecasters do update more often than others — and how frequently a forecaster updated was among the best predictors of accuracy — but the updates themselves are characteristically modest, a nudge of three or four percentage points rather than a lurch from 20% to 80%.

Tim Minto, one of the tournament's top performers with a Brier score around 0.15, exemplifies the style. On a single question about Syrian refugee numbers he revised his estimate dozens of times, usually by small increments, tracking the evidence like a sailor making constant minor corrections to stay on course. Each revision was individually unimpressive; collectively they kept his probability tightly coupled to reality.

Two caveats keep this from becoming a mechanical recipe. First, updating cannot compensate for a careless opening estimate: superforecasters' initial forecasts were already about 50% more accurate than regular forecasters', so the Fermi-izing and outside-view work described earlier still does the heavy lifting. Second, frequency alone is not the skill. Anyone can fiddle with numbers daily; the hard part is judging when the news warrants movement and how much. That judgment is where forecasters fail in two mirror-image ways — reacting too little or too much — and the rest of this chapter is about threading between them.

There is also a quiet mathematical benefit to gradualism. If each update contains some error, many small steps let those errors partially cancel, whereas one dramatic swing bets everything on a single reading of ambiguous evidence. Small steps are a hedge against your own fallibility.
Underreaction: Why Beliefs Defend Themselves

The oldest obstacle to updating is belief perseverance — the tendency to cling to a conclusion after its evidentiary support collapses. Leon Festinger's classic study of a doomsday cult showed the extreme case: when the prophesied apocalypse failed to arrive, committed members did not abandon the belief; they reinterpreted the failure as confirmation that their faith had saved the world. Everyday versions are gentler but pervasive: we rationalize, reinterpret, or simply ignore inconvenient facts.

Two forces supercharge the effect. The first is commitment. Once you have stated a forecast — especially publicly, especially professionally — changing it feels like admitting error, so the stakes of updating are no longer just accuracy but reputation and ego. The more a person has invested in a judgment, the more evidence it takes to move them, which is precisely backward.

The second is identity. Some beliefs are not mere factual estimates but badges of belonging to a tribe — psychologists call the resulting motivated reasoning identity-protective cognition. Dan Kahan's experiments show its power: when a data table was framed as being about a neutral topic (a skin-cream trial), more numerate people read it more accurately; when the identical numbers were framed as gun-control evidence, more numeracy produced more polarization. Intelligence became a weapon for defending the tribe's position rather than a tool for finding the truth.

Superforecasters guard against all this by keeping their self-worth out of their forecasts. A forecast is a provisional probability, not a possession. The ideal is captured in the line attributed to John Maynard Keynes: "When the facts change, I change my mind. What do you do, sir?" The quote is likely apocryphal, but the attitude is the point — and it is rarer than it sounds, because facts that threaten commitments or identities do not feel like facts; they feel like attacks.
Overreaction: Dilution and the Lure of Noisy News

The opposite failure is just as damaging. Forecasters glued to the news flow can treat every headline as signal, when most day-to-day fluctuation — a poll wobble, an official's stray remark, a single month's data — is noise around an unchanged underlying reality. Chasing it produces the forecasting equivalent of the over-trading investor who churns his portfolio on every rumor and underperforms the patient index holder.

Worse, even irrelevant information distorts judgment. In experiments by Henry Zukier and Richard Nisbett on the dilution effect, people asked to predict a student's GPA used genuinely diagnostic information sensibly — until researchers added useless details (his hobbies, how many plants he kept). The irrelevant facts diluted the diagnostic ones, dragging predictions toward a mushy average. More information made judgments worse, because the mind weighs the whole impression rather than filtering for relevance.

This creates a real tension for forecasters, who are told both to stay hungry for information and to hold their estimates steady against noise. The resolution is not a slogan but a filter question applied to each new item: does this actually change the probability of the outcome, or does it merely change how the situation feels? A vivid but non-diagnostic story should move you zero. A dry but diagnostic statistic may deserve real weight.

The small-increment habit doubles as insurance here too. A forecaster who caps most moves at a few percentage points cannot be whipsawed by any single noisy headline, yet still accumulates genuine signal across many updates. Underreaction and overreaction are both errors of weighting — and the discipline that fixes weighting has a name three centuries old.
Bayes in Spirit: Weight Evidence by Its Diagnosticity

Bayes' theorem, developed by the eighteenth-century minister Thomas Bayes and published posthumously by Richard Price, formalizes rational updating: your posterior belief should equal your prior belief multiplied by the likelihood ratio — how much more probable the new evidence is under one hypothesis than under the alternative. In plain terms: start from where you were (the prior, ideally anchored in a base rate), then move in proportion to how strongly the evidence discriminates between possibilities. Highly diagnostic evidence, big move; ambiguous evidence, small move; irrelevant evidence, no move.

Interviews with superforecasters found that most know the theorem but almost none plug numbers into it. What they share is the Bayesian spirit, and Bill Flack's handling of the Arafat question is the worked example. The tournament asked whether the Swiss or French investigations would find elevated polonium in Yasser Arafat's exhumed remains. Flack had Fermi-ized his way to an initial estimate around 60% (see chapter 3), then updated as events unfolded — including extending timelines when the Swiss team's report was delayed.

Then came a jolt: the head of the Russian forensic team announced that Arafat's remains showed no polonium traces. Headlines screamed; many forecasters slashed their probabilities. Flack instead interrogated the evidence's diagnosticity. The question asked specifically about the Swiss or French teams, not the Russian one; the Russian statement was ambiguous and possibly politically shaded; and it said little about what the named investigations would report. So he adjusted only modestly. Weeks later the Swiss team reported polonium levels that moderately supported the poisoning hypothesis, the question resolved yes, and Flack's disciplined weighting earned an excellent score while the overreactors paid for treating a loud headline as strong evidence.
The lesson generalizes: the size of an update should track the evidential weight of the news, not its volume, vividness, or emotional charge.
When the Right Move Is a Big Leap

Gradualism is a default, not a dogma. Bayes' logic cuts both ways: when evidence is genuinely and powerfully diagnostic, a small polite adjustment is just underreaction wearing the costume of prudence. Superforecasters occasionally make dramatic revisions — 30, 40, 50 percentage points — and being able to leap when the facts demand it separates them from forecasters who mistake stubbornness for steadiness.

Flack again supplies the illustration. Asked whether Japanese prime minister Shinzo Abe would visit the controversial Yasukuni Shrine that year, Flack reasoned from Abe's incentives: a visit would inflame relations with China and South Korea and irritate the United States for little gain, so a rational actor would send a ritual offering instead. He put the probability low. Then a news report indicated Abe actually planned to visit. The report contradicted Flack's tidy rational-actor model — exactly the kind of evidence belief perseverance tells us to explain away — but it was highly diagnostic, direct reporting about the specific event in question. Flack overrode his model and jumped his forecast sharply upward. Abe visited the shrine in December 2013.

The episode captures the full skill. Flack's inside-view logic was reasonable; his willingness to let one strongly diagnostic fact overturn it was better. Good updaters hold their analyses firmly enough to resist noise and loosely enough to abandon them when reality dissents.

So the chapter's title is a policy, not a rule: many small steps because most news deserves only a nudge; few big leaps, reserved for evidence that truly discriminates. Both the stepping and the leaping serve one master — proportioning belief to evidence — and, as the next chapter shows, forecasters get better at that proportioning the same way they get better at anything: deliberate practice with honest feedback.
In practice
Revisit every live forecast you hold on a regular schedule this week, and prefer several small revisions of a few percentage points over one dramatic rewrite.

Before updating on any news item, ask the diagnosticity question: how much more likely is this evidence if the outcome happens than if it doesn't? Move in proportion to the answer, and not at all for vivid-but-irrelevant details.
Write your forecasts down privately before announcing them anywhere, and treat a stated number as a provisional estimate to be revised — never as a position to be defended.
When incoming evidence threatens a belief tied to your politics, profession, or self-image, flag it as an identity-protection risk and deliberately steelman the disconfirming interpretation.
When a single piece of evidence directly and credibly bears on the exact question you are forecasting, override your prior model and make the big jump — gradualism is not an excuse for underreaction.
Anchor each new question in a base-rate prior first, so your subsequent updates have a disciplined starting point to move from.
